content: 80 min read Just now -- Introduction JavaScript is one of the most widely used programming languages in the world. Today, it powers everything from interactive websites and backend servers to desktop applications and even mobile apps. However, JavaScript wasn’t originally designed to be this powerful. In 1995, JavaScript was created by Brendan Eich in just 10 days at Netscape. The language was initially developed to add interactivity to web pages, but over time it evolved into one of the most influential programming languages in software development. This guide explains JavaScript from the ground up, focusing not only on what happens but also why it happens internally. Part 1 — JavaScript Fundamentals What is ECMAScript? One of the biggest misconceptions among beginners is assuming JavaScript and ECMAScript are the same thing. They are closely related, but they are not identical. ECMAScript is the official specification that defines how the JavaScript language should behave. JavaScript is an implementation of that specification. Think of ECMAScript as the blueprint, while JavaScript is one implementation of that blueprint. For example: • ECMAScript defines that let should have block scope. • Chrome’s V8 engine implements that behavior. • Firefox’s SpiderMonkey implements the same behavior. • Safari’s JavaScriptCore also implements it. Every browser follows the ECMAScript specification so that JavaScript behaves consistently across platforms. What is JavaScript? JavaScript is a high-level, lightweight, dynamically typed, prototype-based programming language that supports first-class functions. Unlike languages such as C++ or Java, JavaScript is executed by a JavaScript engine inside an existing runtime environment, such as a web browser or Node.js. Modern JavaScript engines use Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation, meaning the code is compiled during execution for better performance. Why is JavaScript called a lightweight language? The term lightweight does not mean the language is weak or less capable. Instead, it refers to the fact that JavaScript requires very little setup and relatively few system resources. JavaScript is considered lightweight because: • It does not require manual compilation before execution. • It runs directly inside a JavaScript engine provided by the browser or runtime. • It has a relatively small and simple syntax. • It is dynamically typed, reducing boilerplate code. • It is event-driven, making it ideal for interactive applications. • It consumes fewer resources compared to many traditionally compiled languages. For comparison: | Language | Execution Model | Requirements | | ---------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------- | | JavaScript | JIT Compiled / Interpreted | Browser or Node.js runtime | | Java | Ahead-of-Time Compiled | JDK, JVM, compilation | | C++ | Ahead-of-Time Compiled | Compiler, linker, executable | | Python | Interpreted | Python Interpreter |Why is JavaScript called a scripting language? JavaScript is often referred to as a scripting language. A scripting language is generally used to control or automate the behavior of an existing application rather than building an entire operating system or standalone application from scratch. A script is simply a small program executed inside another program. For JavaScript, that host program is usually: • Chrome • Firefox • Edge • Safari • Node.js When JavaScript runs inside a browser, it controls the browser’s behavior rather than creating the browser itself. For example: • Handling button clicks • Updating the DOM • Changing CSS styles • Making API requests • Validating forms • Animating UI components JavaScript manipulates existing HTML elements instead of generating the browser’s rendering engine or DOM implementation Why JavaScript is considered a scripting language? JavaScript satisfies the traditional definition of a scripting language because: • It executes inside another application (browser or Node.js). • It is embedded into HTML to make pages interactive. • It primarily controls existing objects like the DOM, CSS, and browser APIs. • It originally executed line-by-line without producing standalone machine code. Although modern JavaScript engines now use JIT compilation, the language is still historically and conceptually categorized as a scripting language. How JavaScript Executes Inside the Browser When a browser encounters JavaScript, it does not understand the source code directly. Instead, it passes the code to its JavaScript engine. Different browsers have different engines: | Browser | JavaScript Engine | | ------- | ----------------- | | Chrome | V8 | | Edge | V8 | | Firefox | SpiderMonkey | | Safari | JavaScriptCore |The engine then converts your JavaScript into machine code that the CPU can execute. Evolution of JavaScript Execution Originally, JavaScript engines worked purely as interpreters. The process was simple: • Read one line. • Interpret it. • Execute it. • Move to the next line. This approach was easy but relatively slow because every execution required interpretation. Modern browsers use Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation. Instead of interpreting every line repeatedly, the engine identifies frequently executed code and compiles it into optimized machine code during runtime. This dramatically improves performance while maintaining fast startup times. High-Level JavaScript Execution Pipeline When a browser loads a JavaScript file, the engine performs several internal steps. JavaScript Source │ ▼ Tokenization (Lexing) │ ▼ Parsing │ ▼ Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) │ ▼ Bytecode Generation │ ▼ Ignition Interpreter (V8) │ ▼ Runtime Profiling │ ▼ TurboFan Optimizing Compiler │ ▼ Optimized Machine Code │ ▼ CPU ExecutionLet’s understand each stage. 1. Fetch Source The browser downloads the JavaScript file or reads inline scripts. 2. Lexical Analysis (Tokenization) The source code is broken into tokens. For example, let age = 25;becomes tokens similar to: let identifier(age) = number(25);3. Parsing The parser verifies that the syntax is valid. If everything is correct, it creates an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). The AST is a tree representation of your program that describes its structure rather than its text. 4. Bytecode Generation Modern engines typically convert the AST into bytecode. Bytecode is an intermediate representation that is faster to interpret than repeatedly traversing the AST. 5. Ignition Interpreter In Chrome’s V8 engine, the Ignition interpreter begins executing the generated bytecode. This allows JavaScript to start running almost immediately without waiting for expensive optimizations. 6. Runtime Profiling While executing your code, the engine gathers runtime information such as: • Which functions execute most frequently • Data types used • Function call counts • Property access patterns Frequently executed functions are called hot functions. 7. TurboFan Optimization When the engine identifies hot code, it sends it to TurboFan, V8’s optimizing compiler. TurboFan generates highly optimized machine code using the profiling information collected during execution. 8. Machine Code Execution Future executions of the optimized function bypass the interpreter entirely and execute directly as machine code. This provides a significant performance improvement. 9. Deoptimization Sometimes the optimizer makes assumptions. For example: let x = 10;The engine may assume x is always a number. Later: x = "Hello";That assumption becomes invalid. The engine discards the optimized code and falls back to the interpreter before potentially recompiling the function using safer assumptions. This process is known as deoptimization. 10. Garbage Collection JavaScript automatically manages memory. Objects that are no longer reachable are periodically removed from memory by the garbage collector, freeing heap space for future allocations. Just-In-Time Compilation JavaScript is commonly described as a Just-In-Time (JIT) compiled language. Unlike Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiled languages such as C++ or Java, JavaScript performs compilation during execution. Instead of compiling the entire program before it runs, the engine compiles only the portions of code that benefit from optimization. This approach provides an excellent balance between startup speed and runtime performance. Part 2 — Loading JavaScript in the Browser Writing JavaScript is only one part of the story. Before your code can execute, the browser must know when and how to load it. Where you place the When the browser encounters a normal This produces an error because the
